ground_truth_source: /Volumes/transit/Games/1/ (Sporting AC match — cross-game test, NOT Rush)

baseline.notes: Original batch run on sporting_ac with 5 frames (commit 8bf1240): F1 0.153, P 0.733, R 0.085, TP 11/129, FP 4. Catastrophic recall collapse. The coarse VLM pass flagged only 22/672 windows (3.3%) with any event vs Rush's 156/685 (22.8%). Hypothesis under test: denser frame sampling (5->7) recovers brief-event recall. Run 44 on Rush *regressed* with 7 frames (F1 0.409->0.369) due to FP inflation on pose-setup events, BUT sporting_ac's failure mode was UNDER-firing rather than over-firing, so it may respond oppositely.

targets.notes: If density is the right lever for cross-game recall, sporting_ac F1 should jump substantially from 0.153 (modest target: 0.25 via better throw_in/shot_on_target recall). If sporting_ac is also flat or regresses, density is not the mechanism and we should revert to 5 frames and try prompt restructure or multi-crop next.

single_pass_frames.reason: Diagnostic probe: does density help when the failure mode is under-firing on sparse events?

notes: Run #45 (diagnostic, not on Rush benchmark): SAME 7-frame config as Run 44 (commit bb8d4d4), but run against sporting_ac instead of Rush. Purpose: isolate whether denser sampling helps the cross-game recall-collapse failure mode (distinct from Rush's over-firing regression). If yes, we accept the Rush regression and keep 7 frames. If no, revert to 5 and attack the prompt/crops.
